query: most common site of infective endocarditis

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Abscess -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A cardiac abscess is a suppurative infection of the myocardium, endocardium, and native or prosthetic valve tissue. Like other abscesses, it develops either by dissemination from a distant source such as bacteremia or sepsis or by direct extension of a preexisting cardiac infective focus. Infective endocarditis has long been identified as the main cause of the latter. Although the incidence of cardiac abscesses continues to be investigated, it is presumably higher than noted postmortem and is of great importance when deciding the prognosis of and therapeutic strategy for patients. Cardiac abscesses are typically monomicrobial; the most common etiologic agents are Staphylococcus aureus or Escherichia coli . Polymicrobial cardiac abscesses are less typical but do occur . [1] [2] [3] Myocardial abscesses may also form in regions of the heart that have experienced prior damage, such as those affected by recent infarction. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Enterococcus Infections -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence of enterococcal infective endocarditis (IE) is consistently rising, present in 8 to 32% of enterococcal bacteremia cases, and it usually has a subacute course. [38] [39] Interestingly, enterococci was the most common pathogen causing IE in subjects who underwent transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI), probably owing to their advanced age and comorbidities. [40] Infective endocarditis can present with a temperature ≥ 38 degrees C (100.4 degrees F), Osler nodes, Roth spots, Janeway lesions, new heart murmur or worsening of preexisting murmur, hematuria, hematuria, and splinter hemorrhages.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurological Sequelae of Endocarditis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Valvular disease such as rheumatic heart disease and congenital heart abnormalities like a bicuspid aortic valve, ventricular septal defect, coarctation of the aorta are risk factors for infectious endocarditis. Pathogens that account for infectious endocarditis vary and depend on the risk factors including intravenous drug abuse, existing valvular dysfunction, poor oral hygiene, and dental procedure, immunocompromised status, the cardiac implantable electronic device (CIED), or previous history of infectious endocarditis. In infectious endocarditis, neurological complications correlate with the size of vegetation and bacterial load. Among all the causative pathogens, Staphylococci aureus is the most common microorganism of infective endocarditis in the United States and other developed countries. Staphylococci aureus IE is significantly more likely to result in embolic events than other pathogens. Neurological sequelae from IE are as following, Embolic stroke is caused by vegetation debris that lodge in the cerebral blood vessels and ended up resulting in infarction; Transient ischemic attack (TIA) is an episode of neurologic dysfunction attributed to ischemia of brain, spinal cord, or retina; Cerebral hemorrhage Meningitis Brain abscess Toxic encephalopathy Seizure

retrieved_docs.title: Tricuspid Valve Endocarditis -- Etiology -- Role of Specific Pathogens
retrieved_docs.content: Staphylococcus aureus Staphylococcus aureus is the most frequently isolated bacterium in TVIE and is the second leading cause of hospital-acquired bacteremia in the US. The annual incidence of S aureus bacteremia (SAB) varies between 4.3 and 38.2 cases per 100,000 person-years, depending on population demographics. IE occurs in approximately 5% to 30% of these cases, depending on region, patient demographics, and diagnostic methods. For example, Rasmussen et al reported only a 5% prevalence of IE in low-risk patients with S aureus infection compared to 38% in those with high-risk factors. [4] S aureus- endocarditis now constitutes 25% to 30% of all endocarditis cases globally, with methicillin-resistant S aureus (eg, MRSA) comprising approximately 37% of all cases in the US and Brazil. The rise in S aureus endocarditis is primarily attributed to IVDU in urban areas, increased cardiac surgeries and invasive procedures, and the widespread use of intravenous catheters in hospitalized patients. Identifying the entry point for SAB can be challenging, with no apparent source identified in 22% to 48% of cases. This uncertainty is more common in community-acquired than hospital-acquired SAB. Community-acquired SAB is considered an independent risk factor for IE, particularly without other apparent infection sources. Research results have shown variability in the risk of IE in catheter-associated SAB. Joseph et al reported a low likelihood of IE in SAB cases associated with intravenous catheters without other infection sources or intracardiac devices. Conversely, Fernandez Guerrero et al suggested catheter-related SAB may represent an underdiagnosed cause of IE, especially with right-sided valve involvement. In these cases, clinical signs may be subtle, necessitating a high index of suspicion to avoid missed diagnoses. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Interventricular Septum Abscess -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: An interventricular abscess is a rare complication of infective endocarditis. It is more prevalent in developing countries where patients may not have access to antibiotics for simple bacteremic infections that seed the cardiac structures and later lead to infective endocarditis. In the U.S. and Europe, patients often have predisposing medical conditions or devices that increase the chance of developing an infection. Among microbiological etiologies, Staphylococci are the leading cause of infective endocarditis, producing a septal abscess. The incidence of infective endocarditis between 2000 and 2011 in the United States increased from 11 per 100,000 to 15 per 100,000 individuals. [13] It is challenging to state a precise incidence of interventricular abscess because the criteria for diagnosing infective endocarditis continue to evolve. The variability of risk factors, predisposing medical conditions, microbiological agents, valvular pathologies, the use of intravenous illicit drug use, and socioeconomic factors are also possible causes of an increasing incidence of the interventricular abscess with infective endocarditis.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Endocarditis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial endocarditis refers to infection of the endocardial surface of the heart. It usually involves heart valves, but it can occur on the endocardium or intracardiac devices. There are two types: Acute endocarditis is a febrile illness that rapidly damages cardiac structures and spreads hematogenously which can progress to death within weeks if not treated. Subacute endocarditis has a slower disease process and may be present for weeks to months with gradual progression unless complicated by major embolic event or ruptured structure. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Aortic Valve Endocarditis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Infective endocarditis occurs globally and is the infection of the endocardial surface of the native valve, prosthetic heart valve, or an implanted cardiac device such as a permanent pacemaker or a cardioverter-defibrillator. [1] Over the past few decades, there has been a change in both the host and the pathogen. Staphylococcus aureus has surpassed Streptococcus as the most common etiologic pathogen worldwide and especially in the developed world given its common association with healthcare contact and invasive procedures. Changes in the host include older and sicker patients who carry a high burden of comorbidities. Resistance to antibiotics is another challenge threatening healthcare worldwide and seems to be growing.

retrieved_docs.title: Endocarditis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Infective endocarditis remains high. Morbidity occurs in more than half of children with a diagnosis of infective endocarditis. Most common complications are heart failure secondary to vegetation in the aortic or mitral valve, myocardial abscesses, toxic myocarditis, and life-threatening arrhythmias. Serious complications are systemic emboli, especially affecting the central nervous system. Other complications include a mycotic aneurysm, acquired ventricular septal defect, and heart block. Overall mortality rates range from 7-15%.

retrieved_docs.title: Infectious Endocarditis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Infectious endocarditis is the inflammation of the endocardium, the inner lining of the heart, as well as the valves that separate each of the four chambers within the heart. It is primarily a disease caused by bacteria and has a wide array of manifestations and sequelae. Without early identification and treatment, a myriad of intracardiac and far-reaching extracardiac complications can develop. Therefore, careful evaluation, including a thorough history and physical exam, can help diagnoses cases and guide management, limiting mortality and morbidity.

retrieved_docs.title: Endocarditis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: It is common to find infective endocarditis as a complication of congenital heart disease or rheumatic heart disease. However, is important to mention that infective endocarditis can present in children without any abnormal valves or cardiac malformations.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Endocarditis -- Evaluation -- Definite Infective Endocarditis
retrieved_docs.content: Pathologic criteria: Pathologic lesions such as vegetation or intracardiac abscess demonstrating active endocarditis on histology or microorganisms demonstrated by culture or histology of vegetation or intracardiac abscess Clinical criteria: Two major clinical criteria or one major and three minor clinical criteria or five minor clinical criteria

retrieved_docs.title: Staphylococcus epidermidis Infection -- History and Physical -- Infectious Endocarditis
retrieved_docs.content: Staphylococcus epidermidis ranks as one of the most common species to cause infective endocarditis in both the prosthetic valve and the native valve. Up to 40% of cases of prosthetic valve endocarditis (PVE) are due to coagulase-negative staph. [9] Once the bacteria produce biofilm within the cardiac valves, it can accumulate and form vegetations. [10] [11] Patients with endocarditis can present with fever, chills, malaise, night sweats, and dyspnea. [12] On physical exam, the patient can present with cardiac murmurs along with petechiae and/or splinter hemorrhages. Uncommonly, other clinical manifestations include Janeway lesions, Osler nodes, and Roth spots.

retrieved_docs.title: Aortic Valve Endocarditis Surgical Treatment -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Data from the European Infective Endocarditis Registry reveal that the most common clinical signs of IE include fever (77.7%), heart murmur (64.5%), and heart failure (27.2%). [11] Embolic complications were present in 25.3% of patients, while cardiac conduction abnormalities were found in 11.5%. [4] Although classic peripheral stigmata, such as splinter hemorrhages and Osler nodes, are less frequently seen today, they may still occur in severe infections caused by S aureus or subacute IE, typically due to Streptococcus spp. Vascular and immunological manifestations, such as Roth spots and glomerulonephritis, remain common. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Abscess -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac abscesses are rare in the United States. S taphylococcus aureus is the most common pathogen when the disease is present. The mitral valve is most commonly implicated in patients with native valves and heart tissue. A 2009 study by Murdoch et al looked at the presentation and etiology and estimated the patient incidence of infective endocarditis at approximately 15%. Regarding prosthetic tissue or valves, the infection tends to be entirely periannular, extends to the myocardium, and results in paravalvular abscesses from the dehiscence of the valve. [8] [9] The following are organisms noted to be involved in cardiac abscess formation: Staphylococcus aureus Haemophilus species Enterococci Escherichia coli ß-hemolytic streptococci Streptococcus pneumoniae Bacteroides species Parasitic organisms Hydatid cysts Miscellaneous

retrieved_docs.content: Skin Findings These are mostly found in IVDU patients: Injection sites or track marks Localized abscesses or cellulitis

retrieved_docs.title: Aortic Valve Endocarditis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Infective endocarditis can be community-acquired, or it can be related to healthcare exposure. The epidemiological trends and disease patterns of infective endocarditis have evolved across the globe. In developing countries, rheumatic heart disease remains the key risk factor, affected patients are usually younger, and community-acquired, penicillin-sensitive streptococci typically cause the infection. [1] In the developed world degenerative valve disease, malignancy, intravenous drug use, diabetes mellitus, and congenital heart disease are the predominant risk factors, and the mean age of the patients is older than the ’70s. The cases of healthcare-acquired infective endocarditis due to Staphylococcal infection have surged over the past two decades owing to an increase in the use of invasive procedures, cardiovascular implantable electronic devices, long-term intravenous lines, percutaneous, minimally invasive, and prosthetic heart valves. [2] There has been an increased incidence of infective endocarditis after minimally invasive aortic valve surgery or percutaneous transcatheter aortic valve replacement. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic rheumatic heart disease is a persistent rheumatic condition that primarily involves the heart and is closely linked to mitral valve disease, including an unspecified mitral valve disorder that represents rheumatic mitral valve failure. Within the heart, papillary muscles are an anatomic component that may be the target of operations on the mitral papillary muscle, a subtype of left‑ventricular operations and often associated with coronary valve therapy. Bacterial myocarditis is classified among peri‑, endo‑, and myocarditis and is also considered a form of cardiomyopathy. Heart murmurs and the pericardium are common clinical entities that reflect the anatomical and functional relationships of the cardiac structures. A hierarchical network of cardiac procedures is organized around the heart and pericardium. “Other or heart procedures” and the broader category HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES include cardiac pacing (demand pacing, implantation of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ (acute rheumatic endocarditis) are all manifestations of acute rheumatic heart disease that share a common clinical course characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. This abrupt and brief presentation is a key feature across these related conditions, aiding clinicians in recognizing the rapid evolution of rheumatic involvement of the heart’s structures and informing timely diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1782
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الجرثومي) is an overarching infectious cardiac condition that encompasses pericarditis, endocarditis, myocarditis, and cardiomyopathy. These four entities represent inflammation of the pericardium, inflammation of the endocardium, inflammation of the myocardium, and disease of the heart muscle, respectively, and each can arise as a manifestation of bacterial infection of the heart. Understanding that pericarditis, endocarditis, myocarditis, and cardiomyopathy are subcategories of bacterial myocarditis aids clinicians in recognizing the full spectrum of bacterial cardiac involvement and in delivering comprehensive diagnostic and therapeutic care.

Closed heart valvotomy of the mitral valve is listed as a specific heart valve procedure performed on the mitral valve. Inflammatory cardiac conditions are organized as a group of peri‑, endo‑, and myocarditis, with bacterial myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الجرثومي) serving as a parent entity. Overall, the summary highlights the central role of the heart in cardiovascular anatomy, rheumatic valvular pathology, diagnostic imaging, electrophysiologic monitoring, device therapy, and the systemic implications of vascular calcification and cardiac tumors for cerebral perfusion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: Heart valve operations (عمليات صمام القلب) encompass a broad category of surgical interventions on cardiac valves. Within this category, “Other operations on valves of heart” are classified as heart valve operations, and specific procedures such as aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري), mitral valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي), tricuspid valve repair (إعاضة الصمام الثلاثي الشرف), and pulmonary valve repair (إعاضة الصمام الرئوي) are identified as narrower subsets of these operations. The procedure “رأب الصمام القلبي” is also considered a type of heart valve operation. Additionally, more complex intrathoracic procedures on the aortic valve performed with cardiopulmonary bypass are mapped primarily to the broader group of other operations on heart valves. Clinically, both aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري) and mitral valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي) are associated with rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية), highlighting the relevance of rheumatic disease in the etiology and management of valve pathology. Understanding these hierarchical relationships and clinical associations aids clinicians in categorizing valve surgeries, selecting appropriate therapeutic strategies, and anticipating disease‑related complications.

The cardiovascular system is also the anatomic site for disease entities such as posterior diabetic retinopathy, bacterial myocarditis, and central nervous system vasculitis. Bacterial myocarditis is further classified under peri‑, endo‑, and myocarditis and is considered a form of cardiomyopathy. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 882
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) is clinically associated with both aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري) and mitral valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي), reflecting the tendency of rheumatic disease to cause severe damage to these left‑sided heart valves that may ultimately require surgical replacement. All of these valve replacement procedures— aortic, mitral, tricuspid (إعاضة الصمام الثلاثي الشرف), and pulmonary (إعاضة الصمام الرئوي) valve replacements—are specific types of heart valve procedures (عمليات صمام القلب). In addition, excision of a heart valve is another category of heart valve procedures and is classified under the broader group of cardiac excisions (HEART: EXCISIONS). Transcatheter aortic valve implantation (زراعة الصمام الأبهري عبر القسطرة) is also a heart valve procedure, representing a minimally invasive approach that delivers an aortic valve prosthesis via catheter. This technique, as well as right‑heart catheterization, relies on vascular catheters (cévní katetry) as the direct devices used to access the cardiac chambers. Consequently, vascular catheters serve as the access devices for transcatheter aortic valve implantation and are integral to right‑heart catheterization procedures. Together, these relationships illustrate how rheumatic disease can lead to valve pathology requiring various surgical or catheter‑based interventions, and how catheter technology underpins both diagnostic catheterization and minimally invasive valve replacement strategies.

Overall, the knowledge base depicts rheumatic fever and its cardiac sequelae as a tightly connected group of entities—ranging from valve‑specific failures (particularly mitral and tricuspid) to diffuse myocardial and pericardial inflammation—typically presenting abruptly and briefly, with possible simultaneous hepatic and renal compromise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, acute rheumatic myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد), acute rheumatic pericarditis (الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد), and acute rheumatic endocarditis (الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ) all share a clinical course that is marked by sudden onset and/or short duration. This rapid and brief presentation is a common feature of the various acute rheumatic heart disease manifestations, indicating that clinicians should anticipate an abrupt, short‑lived disease course when evaluating and managing these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1448
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is a clinical symptom that can be associated with تضيق الأبهر (aortic stenosis), a cardiac condition characterized by narrowing of the aortic valve and resulting hemodynamic compromise, as well as with داء المبيضات الفموي (oral thrush), an infection of the oral mucosa caused by Candida species. While تضيق الأبهر represents a direct cardiovascular cause of chest discomfort, داء المبيضات الفموي may reflect systemic or immunologic factors that can also manifest with chest pain, highlighting that nonspecific chest pain may arise from both cardiac and non‑cardiac origins. Recognizing this dual association is important for clinicians to conduct a thorough evaluation and to tailor management strategies appropriately.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 942
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac valve procedures (عمليات صمام القلب) encompass a range of surgical interventions that address dysfunction of the heart’s semilunar and atrioventricular valves. Specific procedures classified under this broader category include aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري), mitral valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي), tricuspid valve repair (إعاضة الصمام الثلاثي الشرف), pulmonary valve repair (إعاضة الصمام الرئوي), and valve stenosis (رأب الصمام القلبي). Both aortic valve replacement and mitral valve replacement are clinically associated with rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية), reflecting the disease’s potential to cause severe valvular damage that often necessitates surgical replacement of the aortic and mitral valves. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians understand the etiologic role of rheumatic fever in valvular pathology and guides appropriate referral to specialized cardiac valve surgery for affected patients.
